query: laboratory findings showing metabolic acidosis due to metformin use

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with MALA may be critically ill and unable to provide history, so broad laboratory testing is reasonable. MALA is also a diagnosis of exclusion, so other etiologies of elevated lactate levels and metabolic acidosis should be ruled out first. Initial testing should include a point of care blood glucose, blood gas analysis to assess the acid-base status, basic metabolic profile to assess the patient for electrolyte abnormalities, bicarbonate level, renal function, and lactate level.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A metformin level can be obtained if there is a concern for metformin toxicity based on history and labs. Metformin levels are usually a send-out to a reference lab. As such, they are not immediately useful in determining the etiology of the patient’s presentation, and the levels do not always correlate with the severity of MALA. Nevertheless, a level may be obtained to inform downstream patient care and determine the etiology of an acute presentation of lactic acidosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A broad workup to evaluate for other etiologies of metabolic acidosis and shock states should be initiated

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There are two subsets of metformin toxicity: Chronic or incidental MALA - a predisposing pathophysiological condition that leads to supra-therapeutic accumulation of metformin Intentional or acute metformin-induced lactic acidosis (MILA) – an acute overdose of metformin

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for MALA includes other causes of hyperlactatemia and metabolic acidosis. MALA is a diagnosis of exclusion and investigation, and initial treatment should be aimed at excluding the etiologies mentioned below.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients at risk for the development of MALA should be observed for a minimum of 12 hours

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: If the clinical status permits history taking, it is important to determine the amount of metformin ingested, and the time it was ingested. In addition, determine any coingestants that may have been taken. A comprehensive medication history should be performed to identify any other anti-hyperglycemic medications. Certain formulations of metformin are combined with other anti-hyperglycemic agents; most concerning are sulfonylureas. Intentional overdose of these medications can present with both MALA and hypoglycemia. Psychiatric history and intent of the ingestion should also be pursued to determine the need for psychiatric consultation and intervention after medical stabilization and clearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin associated lactic acidosis (MALA) is defined as pH < 7.35 and lactate > 5 mM in the setting of known or suspected metformin use

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium bicarbonate infusion is recommended for pH < 7.20, especially in the setting of underlying cardiovascular disease or hemodynamic compromise

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Methylene blue may be used adjunctively as a possible rescue agent for vasoplegic shock and metabolic bypass

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin overdose induces two distinct metabolic derangements – hyperlactatemia and metabolic acidosis – primarily through inhibition of aerobic respiration.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis should be initiated for pH < 7.0, lactate > 20, and in patients who do not respond to more conservative therapies

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: MALA occurs in about 9% of mono-overdoses of metformin and 0.7% of poly-substance overdoses

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Supratherapeutic metformin levels contribute to hyperlactatemia and separately a metabolic acidosis through impairment of oxidative phosphorylation. [5] There are two subsets of metformin toxicity, chronic or incidental MALA and intentional or acute metformin-induced lactic acidosis (MILA). These definitions are often blurred. In the incidental variant of MALA, a predisposing pathophysiological condition leads to supra-therapeutic accumulation of metformin in the bloodstream and subsequent hyperlactatemia and metabolic acidosis. [6] This typically occurs in the setting of acute or chronic medical comorbidities, including renal injury, hepatic or heart failure, shock, critical illness, or in the presence of other coingestants. In MILA, acute metformin overdose and supratherapeutic drug levels primarily drive the sequelae of hyperlactatemia and metabolic acidosis. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is a biguanide compound used as an antihyperglycemic in the treatment of diabetes mellitus

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physical exam should begin with overall visual assessment and evaluation of airway, breathing, and circulation (ABCs). Signs of MALA are non-specific but can be similar to acidemia from other conditions, which may include tachypnea, hyperpnea, and tachycardia. [20] Severe acidemia may impair myocardial contractility and catecholamine binding, resulting in shock and hypothermia.

Severe diabetic vascular disease can lead to lower‑extremity amputation (بتر الطرف السفلي), subdivided into foot amputation (بتر القدم) and above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة), often accompanied by tissue necrosis and necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb (linked to النَخَر tissue pattern). retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1008
retrieved_docs.content: Fenformin is identified as a member of the biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drug class. Clinical information links fenformin to نقص تركيز السكر (low blood‑sugar concentration). In turn, فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyper‑kalaemia) is clinically associated with نقص تركيز السكر, indicating that episodes of low blood‑sugar may coincide with elevated serum potassium levels. Additionally, الحماض (acidosis) is noted to co‑occur with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم, suggesting that hyper‑kalaemia often appears together with acid‑base disturbances. Together, these relationships highlight a cascade in which fenformin use can be related to hypoglycaemia, which may be associated with hyper‑kalaemia, and that hyper‑kalaemia frequently accompanies acidosis, underscoring the importance of monitoring electrolyte and acid‑base status in patients receiving biguanide therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is classified as a member of the biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drug class, which comprises agents used to reduce blood glucose levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

Hyperglycemia/new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد) is placed within the dehydration group and includes polyuria (تأثير إدرار البول) as a characteristic feature. Overall, the knowledge base underscores the interdependence of lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, electrolyte and acid‑base homeostasis, endocrine‑pancreatic function, renal health, respiratory and hepatic pathology, and substance‑use effects, highlighting the necessity of integrated assessment and multidisciplinary management for patients presenting with these overlapping conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2606
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is categorized under other endocrine disorders, indicating that it is considered a component of the broader group of endocrine system diseases. Clinically, hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is associated with hypoglycemia, suggesting that patients who experience low blood‑sugar levels may also develop elevated serum potassium. In addition, hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض), reflecting a common electrolyte and acid‑base disturbance that can accompany high potassium levels. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating electrolyte balance and acid‑base status when managing endocrine disorders such as hypoglycemia, as concurrent hyperkalemia and acidosis may influence diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2650
retrieved_docs.content: The class of biguanide blood glucose‑lowering drugs includes both fenformin and metformin, each of which is a member of this drug category that is used to lower blood glucose levels in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1597
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that include electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) as a specific subset. Electrolyte imbalance, in turn, comprises conditions such as increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية) and hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم). Additionally, fluid and electrolyte disorders are linked to diabetic hyperosmolar coma (غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري), underscoring the critical role of precise fluid and electrolyte management in patients with severe hyperglycemia. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians identify and treat related metabolic abnormalities more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is classified as a subgroup of other endocrine disorders and encompasses several specific conditions. Among its child entities are مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia), اضطراب إفراز الجسترين (gastrin‑secretion disorder), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is recognized as a manifestation of pancreatic endocrine disease and, in the broader endocrine context, is considered a related disorder within the spectrum of other endocrine disorders. The gastrin‑secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين) can lead to downstream gastric pathology, specifically الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities). Pharmacologically, fenformin (metformin) is associated with a relationship to hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر), reflecting its glucose‑lowering effect and the potential for low blood‑sugar episodes in susceptible patients. Together, these interconnections illustrate how diseases of the pancreatic endocrine system are nested within the larger category of endocrine disorders, how individual pancreatic conditions influence related metabolic and gastrointestinal outcomes, and how therapeutic agents such as fenformin interact with these pathophysiological processes.

This disease group itself is a child of the broader category “other endocrine disorders,” which also encompasses hypoglycemia as one of its subordinate conditions. Gastrin‑secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين) further gives rise to gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities (الحثل واضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ), reflecting the downstream impact of altered gastrin levels on the gastrointestinal mucosa. Metformin (fenformin) is linked to hypoglycemia, indicating that while metformin primarily reduces hepatic glucose production, it may contribute to low blood glucose under certain circumstances, reinforcing the need for careful monitoring when used together with insulin therapies such as insulin aspart. Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) co‑occurs with diabetes, acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), dehydration (التجفاف), acidosis, respiratory depression (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي), abnormal mental states, and low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم). Acute pancreatitis also predisposes to acute kidney failure. Hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with metabolic acidosis and with hypoglycemia, the latter being linked to the antidiabetic drug metformin (fenformin). Acute kidney injury (AKI) is clinically associated with hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hypovolemia, hyponatremia syndrome, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes; AKI may progress to uremic toxicity (extrarenal nitrogen accumulation). Urinary tract disorder is a parent condition of AKI and is related to cystic disease (الدَّاءُ السِّيسْتينِي); pyelonephritis is associated with hyponatremia. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: and the insulin secretagogue repaglinide. The “biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs” subclass comprises fenformin and metformin, with metformin explicitly identified as a biguanide. Lipid‑modifying therapy is represented by the plain fibrate class, which contains bezafibrát, klofibrát and gemfibrozil. Bezafibrát and klofibrát each produce decreased lipolysis, contributing to reduced circulating free fatty acids and improved lipid profiles. Gemfibrozil is linked to the formulation group “G [Preparations]” as its parent preparation and is contraindicated when co‑administered with repaglinide, highlighting a clinically important drug‑interaction risk in patients receiving both lipid‑lowering and glucose‑lowering treatments. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications delineate the specific agents within each diabetes‑related class, their molecular targets and physiologic effects, and key therapeutic interactions that inform safe and effective patient care.
